coredata.dc:description: A highly sensitive ratiometric fluorescence sensing system was developed for simultaneous glucose and total cholesterol (TC) detection in whole blood using MnFe-layered double hydroxides (MnFe-LDHs) as a peroxidase mimic, combined with an o-phenylenediamine (OPD) substrate and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs). The detection platform, an X-shaped laminated microfluidic paper-based analytical device (XL-μPAD), was fabricated via laser printing and cutting. The MnFe-LDHs' large surface area and layered structure provide a high affinity for OPD, with a Michaelis–Menten constant (KM) of 0.0127 mmol L−1. Upon placing a drop of blood on the XL-μPAD sample pad, the enzymatic reactions of glucose and TC produce H2O2, which MnFe-LDHs convert to hydroxyl radicals (•OH). These radicals oxidize OPD into fluorescent 2,3-diamino phenazine (DAP) with emission at 560 nm. Meanwhile, the N-GQDs emit fluorescence at 415 nm, which is quenched by DAP through the inner filter effect (IFE) and dynamic quenching, enabling ratiometric sensing via the intensity ratio (I560/I415). As H2O2 levels increase, a visible green emission appears, correlating with glucose and TC levels. This XL-μPAD system demonstrates promising potential as a portable device for multiplex biomarker detection and diagnostic applications.

Inset: photographs of the corresponding solutions.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of (A) N-GQDs, (B) N-GQDs+OPD, (C) N-GQDs+MnFe-LDHs, (D) N-GQDs+MnFe-LDHs+OPD, (E) N-GQDs+MnFe-LDHs+OPD+H2O2 mixtures; (b) absorption spectra of N-GQDs (solid blue curve) and DAP (solid yellow curve), and the fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs (dotted blue curve) and DAP (dotted yellow curve); (c) absorption spectra of DAP and a mixture of N-GQDs+DAP; (d) Zeta potential of N-GQDs and DAP; reaction time was 5 minutes in sodium citric acid buffer at pH= 5, with concentrations of 50 mg L−1 MnFe-LDHs, 5 mmol L−1 OPD, and 10 mmol L−1 H2O2, excitation at 340 nm. The error bars represent the standard deviation (SD) of three repeated measurements (n = 3). The fluorescence spectra were recorded with 340 nm excitation.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the MnFe-LDHs/OPD/H2O2/N-GQDs system with various concentrations of H2O2 from 0–10 mmol L−1 (0, 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, and 10 mmol L−1) (assuming H2O2 as the limiting agent, the DAP concentration was calculated); (b) Stern-Volmer plot of I0/IF versus [DAP] (I0 and IF represent the fluorescence intensities of I405 nm in the absence and presence of DAP); reaction time was 5 minutes in sodium citric acid buffer at pH= 5, with concentrations of 50 mg L−1 MnFe-LDHs, 5 mmol L−1 OPD under excitation at 340 nm (n = 3).

Linear calibration plots for fluorometric detection of (a) glucose and (c) TC using MnFe-LDHs as peroxidase mimic. Inset: (a,c) pictures of the color change of paper reaction zones (diameter 0.5 nm) (n = 3).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly sensitive ratiometric fluorescence sensing system was developed for simultaneous glucose and total cholesterol (TC) detection in whole blood using MnFe-layered double hydroxides (MnFe-LDHs) as a peroxidase mimic, combined with an o-phenylenediamine (OPD) substrate and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs). The detection platform, an X-shaped laminated microfluidic paper-based analytical device (XL-μPAD), was fabricated via laser printing and cutting. The MnFe-LDHs' large surface area and layered structure provide a high affinity for OPD, with a Michaelis–Menten constant (KM) of 0.0127 mmol L−1. Upon placing a drop of blood on the XL-μPAD sample pad, the enzymatic reactions of glucose and TC produce H2O2, which MnFe-LDHs convert to hydroxyl radicals (•OH). These radicals oxidize OPD into fluorescent 2,3-diamino phenazine (DAP) with emission at 560 nm. Meanwhile, the N-GQDs emit fluorescence at 415 nm, which is quenched by DAP through the inner filter effect (IFE) and dynamic quenching, enabling ratiometric sensing via the intensity ratio (I560/I415). As H2O2 levels increase, a visible green emission appears, correlating with glucose and TC levels. This XL-μPAD system demonstrates promising potential as a portable device for multiplex biomarker detection and diagnostic applications.

ce:keyword.ce:text: X-shaped laminated microfluidic paper-based analytical device (XL-μPAD)

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Abnormal levels of glucose and total cholesterol (TC) in the blood significantly elevate the risk of cardiovascular diseases, cerebrovascular disorders, and diabetes [1]. Additionally, it has been established that high glucose levels are a major contributor to atherosclerosis by promoting cholesterol accumulation in macrophages [2,3]. Particularly, high levels of both glucose and TC can cause severe conditions such as atherosclerosis, which are potentially linked [4]. Maintaining a healthy life is achieved through controlling and managing clinical analytes like cholesterol and glucose levels in human body fluids [5]. Glucose levels are categorized into three stages: normal (≤100 mg/dL or 6.11 mmol L−1), borderline (100 −125 mg/dL or 6.11 −6.94 mmol L−1), and high (i.e., hyperglycemia; >126 mg/dL or 6.99 mmol L−1). In serum, TC comprises approximately 30 % free cholesterol and about 70 % cholesteryl esters. TC is typically categorized into three levels: normal (≤200 mg/dL or ≤5.17 mmol L−1), borderline (200 −239 mg/dL or 5.17 −6.19 mmol L−1), and high (>240 mg/dL or >6.21 mmol L−1) [3,4]. Therefore, monitoring both glucose and TC levels is essential for effective health management and disease prevention.

The routine patterns of clinical analyses of both glucose and TC are determined based on a colorimetric enzymatic reaction. These methods generally employ specific reagents, including glucose oxidase (GOx), cholesterol esterase (ChE), cholesterol oxidase (ChOx), peroxidase, and a chromogenic substrate coupled with an autoanalyzer. In both cases, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is generated as an intermediate product. The H2O2 was catalyzed by the horseradish peroxidase enzyme (HRP) in the presence of a chromogenic substrate, producing a colored product, which is directly proportional to the concentration of glucose or TC [6,7].

These methods have several limitations, including multiple steps in sample preparation, high instrumentation costs, time-consuming detection processes, the requirement to operate at 37 °C, and the necessity for skilled operators to handle the equipment. The use of HRP has several drawbacks, including its high cost, the need for storage under strictly controlled conditions, and its susceptibility to denaturation in extreme environments, such as high temperatures or strongly acidic or basic conditions [8]. Researchers have focused on developing nanomaterials that serve as peroxidase enzyme substitutes for HRP. These nanomaterials replicate enzymatic activity and exhibit catalytic performance comparable to HRP in biosensing applications. They offer several advantages over natural enzymes, including enhanced stability and durability under harsh conditions [9]. Nanomaterials mimicking peroxidase activity, mainly carbon magnetite nanoparticles - [10,11], carbon nanodot-[12,13], and metal- [14–17], have been designed for use as peroxidase mimics for determining glucose and TC. Our previously reported vanadium-doped porous Co3O4 was used as a peroxidase mimic for simultaneous glucose and TC determination [18]. However, these nanomaterials have limitations, such as complicated synthesis methods, expensive raw materials, and low catalytic activity. Nanomaterials are attracting significant interest because of their exceptional structure and properties. In particular, layered double hydroxides (LDHs), known for their large surface areas and layered structures, have garnered significant attention for biomedical applications [19,20]. Generally, manganese (Mn) is closely linked to the active site or functions as a cofactor in various enzymes, such as manganese metalloporphyrins, as epoxidation catalysts [21]. Manganese could act like a peroxidase enzyme mimic (same as a ferric ion in the HRP structure). In recent years, some MnFe-LDHs materials having peroxidase-mimetic catalytic activity similar to biological enzymes have been reported [22–24]. Composite materials such as upconversion nanoparticles on FeMn-LDHs nanosheets [23] and MnFe-LDHs nanosheets [24] can catalyze the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetra-methylbenzidine (TMB), o-phenylene-diamine (OPD) and other chromogenic reagents by H2O2, resulting in color production. To our knowledge, no MnFe-LDHs have been reported as peroxidase mimics for glucose and TC determination.

Fluorescence assays have garnered significant attention due to their exclusive dependability on the signal-to-background ratio or the fluorescence intensity reading during analysis. The sensitivity achieved through this method is typically two to five times greater than that of traditional chromogenic substrates used in colorimetric assays [25]. Fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs) are excellent luminescent materials due to their advantages, including simple synthesis, ease of functionalization, good dispersibility, high fluorescence quantum yield, and good biocompatibility [26]. Nevertheless, most traditional fluorescent sensing systems depend on a single emission signal, which can be affected by various analyte-independent factors, such as instrument deviations, environmental fluctuations, variations in probe concentrations, and the sample matrix [27,28]. To avoid such issues, ratiometric fluorescent sensors simultaneously capture emission intensities at multiple wavelengths, and the resulting intensity ratios are calibrated for analyte quantification. Unlike the single emission sensing mode, ratiometric fluorescent sensing offers built-in correction, which reduces interference, improves signal-to-noise ratios, and enhances the accuracy of the analysis [29,30]. The fluorescent signal ratios exhibit multicolor variations during the sensing process, allowing for direct visual discrimination with the naked eye [31].

Herein, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on the use of MnFe-LDHs as a peroxidase mimic with OPD substrate and N-GQDs for simultaneous glucose and TC detection in a whole blood sample with an X-shaped laminated microfluidic paper-based analytical device (XL-μPAD) was developed ( Scheme 1). With an intrinsic peroxidase-like activity, the prepared MnFe-LDHs can be combined with the N-GQDs to develop a ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategy to detect glucose and TC. After the enzymatic reactions, the generated H2O2 is oxidized by MnFe-LDHs to produce •OH, which can oxidize OPD to form a fluorescent 2,3-diamino phenazine (DAP) product with a strong yellow-fluorescence emission at 560 nm. The generated DAP simultaneously quenches the blue fluorescence of N-GQDs at 415 nm through the inner filter effect (IFE) and dynamic quenching. The fluorescence intensity ratio of DAP/N-GQDs (I560/I415) increases with an increase in H2O2 concentration, leading to the development of a ratiometric fluorescent sensor. Increased levels of H2O2 resulted in a slight green color emission from mixing blue and yellow colors corresponding to glucose and TC levels. The XL-μPAD was fabricated by laser printing and cutting techniques. The simultaneous ratiometric fluorescence-based determination of glucose and TC was investigated by applying a single drop of whole blood onto the sample zone, with the serum being subsequently separated from the whole blood. The serum flowed through the paper device to the four detection zones, where the specific enzymes, OPD substrates, and MnFe-LDHs were coated. After 10 minutes, the N-GQDs solution was added to the detection zones. The color emission was generated in the detection zone. ImageJ software was used to capture the picture and analyze the intensity using the Red, Green, and Blue (RGB) system. The N-GQDs-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor was constructed for sensitive and selective detection of glucose and TC using MnFe-LDHs as peroxidase mimics with OPD as substrate. Additionally, the developed XL-μPAD was successfully employed for simultaneous glucose and TC detection in whole blood samples. The XL-μPAD exhibits advantageous characteristics, including low cost, high efficiency, good accuracy, excellent sensitivity, and desirable selectivity, making it a promising tool for point-of-care diagnostics in medical technology.
Details on the chemicals, apparatus, and instruments, synthesis of MnFe-LDHs, and synthesis of N-GQDs are provided in the Supplementary material (SI).

The desired pattern was created using Microsoft PowerPoint. The design was then printed on Whatman No. 1 filter paper using a laser printer with “printing on both sides” format to create the hydrophobic barrier. Then, the hydrophobic barrier was melted into the thickness of the paper through heating [32,33]. As in our previous study, the optimum baking conditions are 165 °C for 15 minutes.

Starting with the laser-printed paper, the alignment and composition of XL-μPAD are schematically shown in Scheme 1 A. First, the LF1 blood separation membrane was punched using a handcraft puncher with a diameter of 9 mm. The detection layer contained a central sample zone (diameter 5 mm) and a cross-shaped flow path (width 1 mm, length 4 mm) to split the sample solution into four detection zones (diameter 6 mm). Finally, the lamination sheet with a 6 mm hole punch was assembled with the blood separation membrane and laser-printed paper using a hot laminator, resulting in an XL-μPAD ready for application. The procedure for the XL-μPAD fabrication is presented in the Supporting video Part I.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescent glucose and TC determination using XL-μPAD
ce:section.ce:para: The glucose detection zone on the XL- μPAD was sequentially coated with 1.5 µL of 5 % APTES, 100 mg L−1 MnFe-LDHs (dissolved in 60 % ethanol), 15,000 U L−1 GOx and 200 mmol L−1 OPD (dissolved in 75 mmol L−1 sodium citrate buffer, pH=5), respectively. The TC detection zone was sequentially coated with 1.5 μL of 5 % APTES, 100 mg L−1 MnFe-LDHs, 10,000 U L−1 ChE, 15,000 U L−1 ChOx, and 200 mmol L−1 OPD, respectively. Then, 25 μL of standard glucose or cholesterol solution was introduced and incubated for 10 minutes. After that, 1.5 µL of 1000 mg L−1 N-GQDs was applied to the same detection zones and allowed to dry at room temperature for another 10 minutes. Finally, under a controlled lighting system with a 365 nm UV lamp, the change in the fluorescence emission on the detection zones was recorded using a smartphone. Then, the intensity was measured using ImageJ software and further used to construct calibration curves for glucose and TC detection using Microsoft Excel, with G-G0 on the y-axis and concentration on the x-axis. G and G0 are green intensities in the presence and the absence of analyte (glucose and TC), respectively (Supporting video part II).

Whole blood samples were obtained from volunteers with the approval of the Human Research Ethics Committee at Ubon Ratchathani University (No. UBU – REC – 141/ 2564). For real sample analysis, 25 μL of whole blood was applied to the center of an XL-μPAD, which was constructed using the LF1 blood separation membrane to separate the plasma from the whole blood. The plasma separated from whole blood flowed along the XL-μPAD to the four detection zones. For the image analysis, the procedures followed were identical to those described for standard glucose and TC determination in the previous paragraph. The detailed procedure for the simultaneous determination of glucose and TC using the XL-μPAD is illustrated in Scheme 1C and demonstrated in Supporting video Part II.

The crystalline structure of MnFe-LDHs was investigated using the X-ray powder diffraction technique (XRD). As presented in Figure S1, the peaks at 11.63°, 19.48°, 36.61°, 47.31°, and 63.17° were indexed to the reflections of (003), (006), (012), (018), and (110) planes, respectively, which are characteristics of the layered structure of LDH [34]. The MnFe-LDHs exhibit intense (012) reflections with an interlayer spacing based on Bragg’s law of 0.248 nm [35], suggesting the sulfate anions (SO4 2−) being located within the interlayer, indicating that MnFe-LDHs were successfully prepared [36,37].

The size and morphology of MnFe-LDHs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Figure S2, the average geometrical size of MnFe-LDHs was 300–400 nm. It can be seen that a large number of nanosheets with irregular shapes assemble into the typical plate-like morphology [38].

The lattice fringes with an interlayer distance of 0.26 nm are close to the 0.45 nm lattice spacing of the (012) planes in anatase MnFe-LDHs (Figure S3a-b), corresponding to the XRD results (Figure S1). The stacked nanosheets of MnFe-LDHs displayed in Figure S3b showed interlayer distance lattice fringes at 0.46 nm, close to 0.45 of the (006) LDHs plane.

Moreover, several particles of MnFe-LDHs perpendicular to the plane are seen as dark lines (Figure S4). The periodic diffraction concentric rings verify the polycrystalline nature of the LDHs by the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern. Five diffraction rings (003), (006), (012), (018), and (110) facets were found, indicating the polycrystalline characteristics of the MnFe-LDHs consistent with the XRD results [34].

Furthermore, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to investigate the oxidation state of Mn and Fe on the MnFe-LDHs material. As shown in Figure S5a, the characteristic peaks of C 1 s, O 1 s, Mn 2p, and Fe 2p were observed at binding energies of 285.100 eV, 530.124 eV, 642.584 eV, and 711.086 eV, respectively, in the survey scan spectrum. The high-resolution scan of the O 1 s spectra (Figure S5b) shows the five peaks of metal–oxide (M-O-M) (528.038 eV), lattice oxygen of the metal oxide (O-M-O; Olatt) (530.124 eV), sulfate (SO4 3−) (531.625 eV), metal- hydroxyl (M–OH) (532.853 eV), and the hydroxyl group (-OH) of absorbed water molecules (534.217 eV). The three peaks of the Mn 2p3/2 spectrum (Figure S5c) at binding energies of 641.218 eV, 642.586 eV, and 644.082 are attributed to the Mn4+ Mn3+ and Mn2+ oxidation states in MnFe-LDHs, respectively. In the Mn 2p1/2 XPS spectrum, three peaks can be fitted with binding energies at 652.414 eV, 653.826 eV, and 655.278, which refer to the Mn4+, Mn3+ and Mn2+ oxidation states in MnFe-LDHs. It is obvious that the content of Mn3+ was significantly higher than Mn2+ and Mn4+. The observation was consistent with the XRD analysis, as trivalent cations are an essential component in hydrotalcite-like compounds [39]. Previous reports on Mn-containing catalysts for oxidation transformations have proposed Mn³ ⁺ as a key catalytic site [40]. Therefore, the presence of Mn³ ⁺ in the MnFe-LDHs could enhance the catalytic performance in the oxidation of H₂O₂ [41]. For the XPS spectra of the Fe 2p3/2 (Figure S5d), three peaks can be observed at binding energies of 710.025 eV, 711.085 eV and 712.655 eV and the other three peaks of Fe 2p1/2 at binding energies of 722.926 eV, 724.225 eV and 725.727 eV, which were related to the Fe2+ and Fe3+ oxidation state in MnFe-LDHs. The above results indicate the multiple element oxidation states of Mn3+/Mn2+ and Fe3+/Fe2+ in the MnFe-LDHs material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of N-GQDs-based ratiometric fluorescence sensing
ce:section.ce:para: In the presence of H2O2, MnFe-LDHs with peroxidase-like activity catalyzed the oxidation of nonfluorescent OPD to the DAP product with a strong yellow-fluorescence emission at 560 nm when excited at 370 nm ( Fig. 1).

The N-GQDs displayed fluorescence emission at 405 nm when excited at 340 nm. As shown in Fig. 2a, the combinations of N-GQDs+OPD (spectrum B) and N-GQDs+MnFe-LDHs (spectrum C) resulted in partial quenching of the fluorescence emission when compared to the unmodified N-GQDs (spectrum A). This is assumed to be due to the π-network of the sp²-bonded N-GQDs providing an effective substrate for anchoring π-conjugated DAP and MnFe-LDH via specific π–π stacking interactions [42]. Meanwhile, focusing on spectra (B) and (C), there are no significant differences in fluorescence emission with intensities of 615 and 586, respectively. In contrast, the MnFe-LDHs/H2O2/OPD/N-GQDs system showed a strong emission of DAP at 564 nm, which decreased the fluorescence of N-GQDs. These findings demonstrated that MnFe-LDHs catalyze the H2O2-mediated conversion of OPD to form DAP, resulting in fluorescence quenching of the N-GQDs. This observation provides the basis for ratiometric fluorescence-based detection of H2O2.

The quenching mechanism of N-GQDs was further investigated by performing UV-Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, and zeta potential measurements. The absorption and emission spectra of individual constituents of the system are shown in Fig. 2b. The maximum absorbance of DAP was observed at 442 nm. Meanwhile, the emission spectra at 405 nm appeared, leading to a spectral overlap between the fluorescence emission of N-GQDs and the absorption band of DAP. This result indicates a high potential for the inner-filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between N-GQDs as donor and DAP as acceptor [43,44]. Next, the absorption spectra of DAP before and after adding N-GQDs show (Fig. 2c) no changes in the position and intensity, confirming that no complex was formed. This result indicated there was no chemical bonding between N-GQDs and DAP [45]. Also, the zeta potential was measured to obtain the surface charges of N-GQDs and DAP. As shown in Fig. 2d, the zeta potential of the dispersed N-GQDs and DAP were −8.945 and −8.76 mV, respectively. This indicates that DAP and N-GQDs are negatively charged; thus, electrostatic interactions could not cause DAP to be adsorbed on the surface of N-GQDs.

[46]. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-GQDs and DAP was caused by the IFE.The fluorescence quenching of N-GQDs by DAP resulted from a specific interaction between N-GQDs and DAP [47]. Furthermore, because DAP is an excellent electron acceptor, the fluorescence of N-GQDs at 405 nm was remarkably decreased [43]. The benzene ring and an amino group of DAP enable interaction with the π-electron systems (graphitic N, oxidized N, and pyrrolic N) in the N-GQD structure through hydrogen bonding and π−π stacking.

The fluorescence quenching response was also analyzed by incubating N-GQDs with different DAP concentrations (0 – 10 mmol L−1). The Stern-Volmer Eqs. (1) – (2) [48]:
(1)

/
I f
= 1 +
K sv

(2)

Where I0 and IF are the fluorescence intensities of N-GQDs before and after the addition of DAP, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and [quencher] is the quencher concentration (DAP), Kq represents the bimolecular quenching constant, and (τ0) is the fluorescence lifetime of N-GQDs.

A linear relationship is generally associated with dynamic quenching, where the quenching is mainly due to collisions between the emitter and quencher molecules [49]. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs (405 nm) decreased gradually with the increase in DAP concentration ( Fig. 3a-b). The ratio of I0 to IF (405 nm) versus the DAP concentration (0–10 mmol L−1) exhibits a linear relationship (I0/IF = 1 + 0.092 [DAP], r2 = 0.9919). KSV can be determined from the slope of the Stern-Volmer plot, providing Ksv = 0.092 mM−1. As the τ0 was determined to be 14.2 ns [50], Kq was then calculated to be 0.65 × 1010 M−1s−1,which was close to the reported maximum value of Kq for the diffusion-controlled quenching process (1.0 × 1010 M−1s−1) [51]. This suggests that the quenching mechanism of N-GQDs by DAP is likely attributed to dynamic quenching rather than static quenching [52]. Overall, the quenching mechanism of N-GQDs involves both dynamic quenching and IFE, as both processes can simultaneously contribute to fluorescence intensity reduction. Dynamic quenching occurs via collisions between N-GQDs and DAP in the excited state, facilitated by hydrogen bonding and π–π stacking, resulting in energy transfer and non-radiative relaxation to the ground state without fluorescence emission. IFE arises when the excitation or emission spectra of N-GQDs overlap with the absorption spectrum of DAP, resulting in the reabsorption of emitted fluorescence. Consequently, fluorescence intensity decreases due to the combined effects of both mechanisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the Peroxidase-Like Activity of MnFe-LDHs
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the peroxidase-like activity of MnFe-LDHs was evaluated through the catalytic oxidation of OPD in the presence of H2O2. The MnFe-LDHs exhibit both oxidase- and peroxidase-mimicking activities, where Mn2+ catalyzes O2 oxidation to generate superoxide anion radicals (O2 •-) (oxidase-like), while in the presence of H2O2, they enhance oxidized product formation (DAP) and hence, fluorescence intensity (peroxidase-like) (The details are provided in the SI). To verify the production of •OH by the MnFe-LDHs, an experiment was conducted utilizing the terephthalic acid (TA) reaction system (details are provided in the SI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of parameters for ratiometric fluorescence sensing of glucose and TC on paper system
ce:section.ce:para: Glucose is oxidized by the GOx enzyme to yield gluconic acid and H2O2. For TC determination, the ChE enzyme converted cholesteryl esters into free cholesterol, which was further oxidized by the ChOx enzyme to produce cholest-4-en-3-one and H2O2. The simultaneous generation of H2O2 in this enzymatic reaction enables a highly sensitive and specific glucose and TC detection method. MnFe-LDHs play a crucial role by catalyzing the oxidation of H₂O₂, resulting in the production of •OH and −OH, which subsequently transform OPD into the yellow fluorescent product, DAP. Ratiometric fluorescence sensing is effective in eliminating the effect of background interference and the surrounding environment [53]. For this purpose, blue fluorescent N-GQDs were added to the detection zone, resulting in a ratiometric fluorescence system (Scheme 1B). The combination of the strongly blue color emitting N-GQDs with the weak yellow color emission from DAP resulted in blue to greenish fluorescence emission corresponding to glucose and TC levels (Figure S12a-b). The ratiometric fluorescent sensor can be accurately fabricated using the two fluorescent signal indicators (DAP and N-GQDs), enabling sensitive and selective TC and glucose determination.

The optimization of reaction parameters for glucose and TC analysis was conducted using a circular paper design with a 5 mm diameter fabricated by laser printing. Optimized parameters included the concentrations of MnFe-LDHs and OPD and the incubation time. The OPD concentration was examined within the range of 50–200 mmol L−1, using an incubation time of 10 minutes, 100 mg L⁻¹ MnFe-LDHs, and 10 mmol L−1 H₂O₂. The results indicate that as the concentration of OPD increases, there is a corresponding increase in the green intensity, and the optimum concentration of OPD was found to be 200 mmol L−1 (Figure S13a). Next, concentrations of MnFe-LDHs as an enzyme mimic ranging from 50 to 400 mg L⁻¹ were studied with a 10-minute incubation time while maintaining H2O2 and OPD concentrations at 10 mmol L−1 and 200 mmol L−1, respectively. The results shown in Figure S13b indicate that a concentration of 100 mg L⁻¹ MnFe-LDHs provided the highest sensitivity and was therefore selected as the optimum concentration. In addition, the incubation time (the MnFe-LDHs catalyzed H2O2 to produce •OH, which can oxidize OPD to generate DAP) was investigated in the range of 5 – 30 minutes. According to Figure S13c, an incubation time of 10 minutes showed the highest G-G₀ values, while longer incubation times resulted in a significant decrease in G-G₀ values. Therefore, 10 minutes of the incubation time was selected for further experiments.

The interaction time (after adding N-GQDs solution into the detection zone) was also optimized by varying it in the range of 5 – 45 minutes for glucose (Figure S14a) and TC (Figure S14b). After dropping 1000 mg L−1 N-GQDs, the G-G0 intensity of the ratiometric fluorescent sensor increased initially and reached a maximum of 10 minutes, which was a complete quenching of the fluorescence. This suggests that the N-GQDs-assisted MnFe-LDHs/OPD system has a prompt response, enabling it to serve as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor [52]. Therefore, 10 minutes was selected for glucose and TC detection to achieve the best sensitivity in the shortest time.

Overall, the incubation time (10 minutes) and interaction time (10 minutes), totaling 20 minutes, were used for the simultaneous fluorometric determination of glucose and TC using the proposed XL-μPAD. In addition, green intensity was used according to color systems to achieve sensitivity and selectivity in chromatic analysis.

In addition, APTES was used to coat the cellulose paper for enzyme (GOx, ChOx, and ChE) and nanomaterial (MnFe-LDHs) immobilization, ensuring uniform color distribution on the detection zone. Specifically, its amino groups are fully protonated at pH < 9.6, thereby increasing the positive charges on the cellulose paper (R-NH3 +) [54]. The isoelectric points (pI) of GOx, ChOx, and ChE are 4.2 [55], 4.7 [56], and 4.5 [57], respectively. The zeta potential of MnFe-LDHs at pH 5 is −11.1 ± 0.28 (n = 3).

At the working pH of 5, APTES carries a net positive charge, while the enzymes and MnFe-LDHs exhibit negative charges since their pI values are lower than the working pH. This difference in charges facilitates strong electrostatic interactions, enabling effective immobilization of the enzymes and MnFe-LDHs on the cellulose paper via non-covalent bonding (e.g., electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding, Van der Waals forces, and hydrophobic interactions).

Additionally, the pKa of DAP is 4.47 [58]. It is partially deprotonated at pH 5 and carries a negative charge. This resulted in higher adsorption of DAP on the surface and uniform color distribution in the detection zone.

The optimum parameters for the fluorometric determination of glucose and TC using MnFe-LDHs are shown in Table S2. As shown in Fig. 4a-d, a linear relationship between G-G0 and glucose and TC concentrations in the range of 0.01 – 10 mmol L−1 was obtained. The detection limits (LOD, calculated from 3 SDblank/slope (n = 3)) for determining TC and glucose were 0.0067 and 0.0071 mmol L−1, respectively.

The results for the simultaneous fluorometric determination of glucose and TC using the proposed XL-μPAD under optimal conditions, applied to aqueous standard solutions, are presented in Figure S15a-b. It was observed that the color intensity increased with higher concentrations of glucose and TC. The eight-point calibration curves for determining glucose and TC covered the concentration ranges of 0.01 −10 mmol L−1 (r2 = 0.9904) and 0.01 −10 mmol L−1 (r2 = 0.9901), respectively. The LOD of determining glucose and TC were 0.0098 and 0.0087 mmol L−1, respectively. The resulting three-point calibration curve within the 1 −10 mmol L−1 range (y = 1.7307x + 20.743, r² = 0.997) provided a LOD of 0.011 mmol L−1 for glucose determination (Figure S16a). For TC determination, the three-point calibration in the range of 0.5 −10 mmol L−1 (y = 1.904x + 17.057, r2 = 0.9988), yielding LOD of 0.0086 mmol L−1 (Figure S16b). Moreover, this sensor provides a similar LOD, a wide linear range, and a user-friendly application interface compared to other methods for monitoring glucose and cholesterol (Table S3). The XL-μPAD system presents several advantageous features, such as a relatively short assay time, quantitatively assessing TC based on clinical criteria, and separate assays for glucose and TC that enable simultaneous testing without cross-reaction between reagents. Importantly, the simultaneous detection of multiple biomarkers increases the accuracy of disease diagnosis and enhances testing efficiency.

The influence of potential interfering substances in whole blood samples was also examined, and the results are presented in Table S4. The interference study for glucose determination included three glucose analogs, maltose, fructose, and sucrose, as well as five potential interfering substances found in the blood: ascorbic acid, uric acid, cholesterol, Ca²⁺, and Na⁺. For TC, the interference study included two substances with similar functional groups to cholesterol, phenol, and glycerin, as well as five potential interfering substances present in blood: glucose, uric acid, ascorbic acid, glutamic acid, cysteine, and tyrosin. The tolerance ratio is calculated as the maximum concentration ratio of each interference to glucose or TC that maintains the G-G0 value within ± 5 % compared to the experiment without interference. The results indicated that the developed XL-μPAD exhibited high tolerance to all tested interferences. Specifically, the tolerance ratios ranged from 0.01 to 10 (with a relative error of −4.29 % to +4.45 %) for glucose determination and from 0.02 to 10 (with a relative error of −4.54 % to +4.76 %) for TC determination. Notably, the lowest tolerance ratios within the specified ranges (0.5 for both glucose and TC) are associated with ascorbic acid. This observation is due to the tendency of ascorbic acid to reduce color intensity at high concentrations, leading to a decrease in the G-G₀ value. Nevertheless, these tolerance ratios remain sufficiently high for determining glucose and TC in human blood samples. The results demonstrate that the developed system exhibits high efficiency in the presence of various potential interferences and shows strong potential for use with human blood samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous determination of glucose and TC in whole blood samples using the developed XL-µPAD
ce:section.ce:para: The potential of developed XL-µPAD for simultaneous glucose and TC detection in whole blood samples was explored. The volume of whole blood was studied, with the results presented in Figure S17. After applying whole blood samples, volumes of 15 and 20 μL were insufficient for the plasma to cover the entire detection area. Conversely, sample volumes of 30 and 40 μL led to the migration of red blood cells (RBCs) into the detection area, which could potentially interfere with the accuracy of the results. Using 25 μL of whole blood demonstrated complete coverage of the detection zone by the plasma without RBCs interfering. As a result, this volume was chosen as the optimal amount for the whole blood sample.

The feasibility of the developed method was further evaluated using whole blood samples with hematocrit values ranging from 31.6 % to 40.0 %. The results indicated that the LF1 blood separation membrane used as a sample pad was adequate for the studied hematocrit percentages, successfully separating plasma from the samples without causing hemolysis. Normal hematocrit levels vary with age and race. In adults, normal hematocrit levels range from 41 % to 50 % for men and 36–44 % for women [59]. As shown in Table S5, the XL-μPAD provides a significant advantage in efficiently separating plasma from blood samples with varying hematocrit levels. The results indicate that whole blood samples with hematocrit values between 31.1 % and 54.3 % can be used with the developed XL-μPAD without requiring any modifications.

The results of seven blood samples analyzed using the proposed XL-μPAD were compared with those obtained using the hospital method, as detailed in Table 1. The percentage relative error was less than 5 % for all samples, indicating that the proposed method provides acceptable accuracy.

In addition, we compared the accuracy of the established eight-point calibration to three-point calibration using our XL-μPAD to quantify glucose and TC. Compared to the hospital method results, the results in Table 1 show that the relative error for all samples was below 5 % calculated with both three-point and eight-point calibration curves, suggesting that the proposed method exhibits acceptable accuracy.

There is no significant difference between using a three-point or eight-point calibration curve for measuring glucose and TC in whole blood samples on the developed XL-μPAD. Thus, the three-point calibration curve shows promise for further development in preclinical testing and point-of-care diagnostics for glucose and TC.

The stability of the developed XL-μPAD was also investigated. The devices were packed in a sealed foil pouch and stored at 4 °C for glucose and TC detection in whole blood samples. The %relative error was below 5 % for all samples, and the color intensity remained stable even after 60 days of storage (Table S6).

Inter-batch precision analysis was also conducted by evaluating the % relative standard deviation (RSD) (n = 3) (%RSD) values obtained from different MnFe-LDHs and N-GQDs synthesis batches. The obtained %RSD values were 4.87 and 3.61 % for determining glucose (Table S7) and TC (Table S8), respectively. These results indicate that the proposed system has high precision and excellent reproducibility.

We synthesized MnFe-LDHs using the co-precipitation method and demonstrated, for the first time, their peroxidase-like catalytic activity for simultaneous glucose and TC detection. Its enhanced catalytic activity is attributed to the significant contribution of metals with peroxidase-like activity and the large surface area and layered structure of LDHs. The synthesized MnFe-LDHs demonstrated several advantages over natural enzymes and other enzyme mimics, such as ease of synthesis, low cost, good stability, and higher substrate affinity. Notably, the MnFe-LDHs exhibited excellent catalytic activity toward OPD. A ratiometric fluorescent sensing approach based on N-GQDs and DAP was successfully developed through dynamic quenching and IFE processes. This approach improves both selectivity and accuracy while reducing matrix interference in whole blood samples. Furthermore, a ratiometric fluorescent sensing method for determining glucose and TC in whole blood was developed, requiring no sample preparation steps and integrating plasma separation from blood samples with varying hematocrit values. Specifically, the prototype XL-µPAD was developed for simultaneous glucose and TC detection at ambient temperature, requiring only a single drop of whole blood (25 µL). The device exhibits high stability, with no performance deterioration observed over a storage period of 60 days. The XL-μPAD platform minimizes reliance on fragile natural enzymes, reduces costs, and facilitates the development of accessible diagnostics for remote or resource-limited settings. The potential application of the system was further evaluated through preclinical testing, which was compared against a conventional method used in hospitals. Overall, the XL-μPAD system has the potential to evolve into a self-monitoring device suitable for both preclinical testing and point-of-care diagnostics for medical technologists. It could be used not only for glucose and TC determination but also for assessing other important biomarkers. This technology supports Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) 3 (good health and well-being) through advancement in diagnostic healthcare and SDG 4 (responsible consumption and production) by supporting the responsible management of chemical waste.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Nattasa Kitchawengkul is a Ph.D. student at the Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ubon Ratchathani University, Thailand under the supervision of Assoc. Prof. Dr. Purim Jarujamrus. Her research interest mainly focused on microfluidic paper-based analytical devices with layered hydroxides and layered double oxides as peroxidase mimetics for fluorescent sensing in multiplex biomarker detection.

Akarapong Prakobkij has just finished a Ph.D. from the Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ubon Ratchathani University, Thailand, under the supervision of Assoc. Prof. Dr. Purim Jarujamrus. His research interest mainly focused on advanced biomarker detection methods: the development of peroxidase mimics and microfluidic paper-based platforms.

Rattaporn Saenmuangchin is a Researcher in Chemistry and Nanotechnology at the National Nanotechnology Center (NANOTEC). Her research interest mainly focused on the developed NanoSampler, an air sampler for collecting various sizes of particulate matters (PMs).

Daniel Citterio is Professor in Analytical Chemistry at the Department of Applied Chemistry of Keio University in Yokohama, Japan. His research interests are the development of low-cost analytical devices and the development of functional molecules for chemical sensing. To date, he has published ∼ 191 articles in journal publications.

Duangjai Nacapricha is an Associate Professor of Analytical Chemistry in the Department of Chemistry, Mahidol University, Thailand. Her research is focused on flow-based analysis, paper-based analytical devices (μPAD), and innovation. To date, she has published ∼ 137 articles in peer-reviewed journals.

Purim Jarujamrus is an Associate Professor at the Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ubon Ratchathani University. His research is focused on developing functional nanomaterials on fiber-based microfluidics analytical devices for novel chemical and bio-sensing and spectroanalytical chemistry. To date, he has published ∼84 articles in peer- reviewed journals.
